task: Design a customer 360 data application for support agents.
assumptions: CRM is canonical for accounts
ticketing system is canonical for support events
support UI tolerates minutes of freshness lag
pattern: Use canonical operational owners, a warehouse/lakehouse analytical layer, materialized support-facing summaries, and reconciliation checks for stale or missing source feeds.
incompatibilities: single canonical write surface for all customer facts

task: Store all product knowledge and permissions only in embeddings for an AI assistant.
assumptions: assistant needs factual permissions and auditability
pattern: Do not use vector retrieval as the source of truth for governed facts; use canonical structured records plus derived retrieval indexes.

task: Model customers, employees, vendors, and account managers for an enterprise CRM.
assumptions: same real-world organization can play multiple roles
names and identifiers may have history
pattern: Use Party as the stable concept, Party Relationship/Role for customer/vendor/employee/manager participation, and separate name/identifier structures for history and issuing authority.
incompatibilities: single-purpose app where separate role tables are intentionally local and non-integrated

task: Build a canonical data model directly around legacy paper forms and report screens.
assumptions: forms summarize or transmit underlying business facts
pattern: Do not treat forms/screens as canonical entities by default; extract the underlying parties, assets, activities, locations, contracts, and information resources.
incompatibilities: the form itself is governed, approved, retained, transmitted, or audited as a business object

task: Design profile updates with read replicas for a global consumer app.
assumptions: writes go to one leader
read replicas may lag
users expect to see their own edits immediately
pattern: Route the writer's session to the leader or a sufficiently caught-up replica, expose follower lag metrics, and reserve stale follower reads for users who did not just write.
incompatibilities: multi-region concurrent edits without conflict semantics

task: Keep a product search index up to date from an operational catalog database.
assumptions: database is canonical for product facts
search index may lag by seconds
full rebuild is feasible
pattern: Use CDC/outbox into a durable log, make index writes idempotent by product/version, monitor lag and dead letters, and retain a full rebuild path from the source of record.
incompatibilities: index is allowed to become canonical source

task: Shard high-volume event ingestion by creation timestamp because dashboards read recent time ranges.
assumptions: writes arrive mostly at current time
ingestion rate is high
pattern: Do not use a purely monotonic timestamp partition key for high write load; combine time bucketing with hashing/salting or separate write-optimized ingestion from time-range analytical layout.
incompatibilities: low write rate archive where simple time partitions are intentionally sufficient

task: Design a small internal expense tracker with simple approvals and reports.
assumptions: business rules are simple
one UI client initially
relational database owns records
pattern: Use presentation/domain/data-source separation, Transaction Script for use cases, and Table Data Gateway or Row Data Gateway to keep SQL out of presentation logic.
incompatibilities: rich domain behavior shared by multiple clients

task: Design a leasing platform with complex pricing, billing, early returns, and rule exceptions.
assumptions: business rules are complex and volatile
relational schema may not match object model
users edit long-running business transactions
pattern: Use a rich Domain Model, Data Mapper, Unit of Work, Identity Map, selective Lazy Load, and optimistic or pessimistic offline locks based on conflict cost.
incompatibilities: one-screen CRUD app where mapping complexity would dominate

task: Scale an enterprise application by putting Customer, Order, Product, and Invoice objects on separate remote servers.
assumptions: objects have fine-grained collaborations
distribution is proposed for scalability rather than a required boundary
pattern: Do not distribute fine-grained domain objects; keep object collaboration local and scale by clustering, adding coarse-grained Remote Facades and DTOs only at unavoidable remote boundaries.
incompatibilities: explicit independent service boundary with coarse-grained API and separate ownership
